name: Loop 12: Col d'Inharpu – Basque Country passes
name_original: Circuit 12 : Col d’Inharpu — Cols du Pays basque
description:
From the authentic village of Mauléon-Licharre, this great loop goes to meet one of the most beautiful passes in the Basque Country. Encircling the forest of Arbailles and the peaks that surround it, it offers many spectacular views.

The start is in the heart of Mauléon-Licharre, capital of the espadrille. A traditional Basque village, its architecture includes Béarnaise-type houses as well as beautiful castles. The route begins by climbing the small Col d'Osquich as a warm-up. Then, between Saint-Just and Lecumberry, a second pass is on the menu, that of Gamia. Very pretty, it offers one of the most beautiful views of the valley. Take advantage of the descent to recover and the village to fill the cans.

From Lecumberry, you begin the 14 kilometer climb that leads to the Inharpu pass. The road climbs gradually on the sides of the Pic de Béhorléguy. This pretty summit accompanies you for a good part of the climb before having Bohorkortea in your sights. The summit of the pass is at the foot of this one. A few meters after the pass is the Auberge d’Ahusquy. Its terrace invites you to take a nice break with a view of the valley.

You then embark on a long descent that plunges into the forest. A few hairpin turns slow the pace before you find the Season. The stream then takes care of bringing you back to your starting point in Mauléon-Licharre.
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Mauléon-Licharre traverse the mountainous terrain of the Basque Country, offering varied elevation profiles. The region is characterized by green valleys, the Pyrenees, and challenging climbs. Cyclists encounter a landscape featuring significant elevation gains, making it suitable for those seeking demanding routes. The area provides a diverse setting for road cycling, from moderate paths to routes with substantial vertical ascent. Best road cycling routes around Mauléon-Licharre The most popular road cycling route is Loop 14: Col de Bagargui from Mauléon – Basque Country passes, a 48.2 miles (77.6 km) trail that takes 4 hours 40 minutes to complete. This route features significant elevation gain through the Basque mountains. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Loop 12: Col d'Inharpu – Basque Country passes, a difficult 46.9 miles (75.4 km) path. This route offers sustained climbing and panoramic views of the surrounding peaks. Local road cyclists also love the Mauléon and Josbaig Valley Loop, a 39.7 miles (63.9 km) trail leading through the Josbaig Valley, often completed in about 3 hours 11 minutes. Road cycling around Mauléon-Licharre is defined by the Basque Mountains, green valleys, and challenging climbs. The network offers options for easy, moderate, and difficult routes, catering to various ability levels. The routes in Mauléon-Licharre are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.25 stars from 4 reviews. More than 400 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Mauléon-Licharre's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Mauléon-Licharre?
A: There are over 25 road cycling routes around Mauléon-Licharre, catering to various skill levels. You'll find a mix of easy, moderate, and challenging options to explore the Basque Country's terrain.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Mauléon-Licharre?
A: The region is ideal for road cycling during the warmer months, typically from spring through autumn. This period offers the most favorable weather conditions for tackling the mountainous terrain and enjoying the scenic views without the challenges of winter conditions.

Q: Are there any easy road cycling routes suitable for beginners in Mauléon-Licharre?
A: Yes, while many routes are challenging, there are 3 easy road cycling routes available around Mauléon-Licharre. These routes offer a gentler introduction to the region's beautiful landscapes, allowing beginners to enjoy the scenery without significant elevation gains.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes in the Basque Country around Mauléon-Licharre?
A: The terrain around Mauléon-Licharre is characterized by its mountainous backdrop, featuring significant elevation gains and challenging climbs. You'll encounter green valleys, winding roads through the Pyrenees, and demanding ascents that require good fitness. The region offers a diverse setting, from moderate paths to routes with substantial vertical ascent.

Q: What are some challenging road cycling climbs or routes in the area?
A: For experienced cyclists seeking a challenge, routes like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1154029794/loop-14-col-de-bagargui-from-mauleon-basque-country-passes" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Loop 14: Col de Bagargui from Mauléon – Basque Country passes&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1153841361/loop-12-col-d-inharpu-basque-country-passes" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Loop 12: Col d'Inharpu – Basque Country passes&lt;/a&gt; offer sustained climbing and panoramic views. The Col d'Osquich is another notable climb, part of the mythical 'Hendaye-Cerbère' traverse, with moderate 7% average gradients over 7 kilometers.

Q: What natural features or viewpoints can I see while cycling around Mauléon-Licharre?
A: Cyclists are treated to breathtaking panoramic views of the Pyrenees and surrounding Basque mountains. You can also explore dramatic natural features like the Gorges de Kakuetta, known for its 'Amazonian atmosphere' and a 20-meter waterfall, or the Holzarte footbridge, suspended 180 meters above a canyon. The Iraty Forest, Europe's largest beech grove, also offers stunning natural beauty.

Q: Are there any historical landmarks or cultural attractions along the cycling routes?
A: Yes, the region is rich in history and culture. You can see the medieval fortified &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5783570" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Château Fort de Mauléon&lt;/a&gt;, offering expansive views of the Saison valley. The 17th-century Château d'Andurain is also nearby. The town of Mauléon-Licharre itself is known as the capital of the espadrille and hosts traditional Basque cultural events. The charming village of &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6723006" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tardets&lt;/a&gt; is also a point of interest.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy the most about road cycling in Mauléon-Licharre?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.25 stars. Reviewers often praise the challenging climbs, the stunning panoramic views of the Pyrenees, and the diverse terrain that caters to various fitness levels, making it a rewarding destination for road cyclists.

Q: Are there any specific loop routes recommended for road cycling?
A: Several excellent loop routes are available. Beyond the popular ones mentioned in the guide's introduction, the 'Tour de Soule' is an 80km loop with 804m of vertical rise, passing through charming villages and green countryside, offering a significant sporting challenge. You can find more details on this route via &lt;a href="https://www.tourisme64.com/en/itinerary/mauleon-licharre/ITIAQU064V509KKY-le-tour-de-soule-cyclotourisme/" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tourisme64.com&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Can I find places to eat or rest along the road cycling routes?
A: Yes, some routes pass through or near villages like Tardets, which has a lively atmosphere. The Col d'Osquich, a notable climb, features a restaurant during its ascent, offering magnificent views of the Soule region below. These spots provide opportunities for refreshments and rest.

Q: Are there any long-distance road cycling trails near Mauléon-Licharre?
A: Yes, the region offers routes that can be combined for longer distances. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/37651845" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Bagargui – Burdincurutcheta Pass loop from Mauléon-Licharre&lt;/a&gt; is nearly 100 km long, providing a substantial ride with significant elevation gain for those seeking an extended challenge.

Q: What is the difficulty level of most road cycling routes in this area?
A: The majority of road cycling routes around Mauléon-Licharre are classified as moderate to difficult. Out of over 25 routes, 10 are moderate and 12 are difficult, indicating that the region is particularly well-suited for cyclists looking for a significant physical challenge and rewarding climbs.
tips: Authentic Basque village, Mauléon-Licharre is considered the capital of the espadrille. It is also that of the small province of the Basque Country, Soule. The heights of its old town are separated from the lower town by the course of the Season. We find in this part the castle of Andurain de Maytie.
A small pass to climb on a fine day, a restaurant on the way up, with a magnificent view of the Soule below.

The small village of Lecumberry is on the road towards Iraty.

The Ibarnia farm serves as an ideal break for refueling.

The road that leads to the Inharpu pass is magnificent. From Lecumberry, the climb is about 14 kilometers at an average of 5.3%. A few meters after the pass is the Auberge d'Ahusquy for a well-deserved break.
